coredata.dc:title: Cascade amplification based on PEI-functionalized metal–organic framework supported gold nanoparticles/nitrogen–doped graphene quantum dots for amperometric biosensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Youxiong
Wei, Xianhu
Gu, Qihui
Zhang, Jumei
Ding, Yu
Xue, Liang
Chen, Moutong
Wang, Juan
Wu, Shi
Yang, Xiaojuan
Zhang, Shuhong
Lei, Tao
Wu, Qingping
coredata.dc:description: Robust and efficient multimodal catalysis platforms that possess high catalytic activity and enhanced electrical conductivity are preferred for enhancing the electrical analysis performance under a low overpotential. This report describes a function-switchable amperometric sensor for electrochemical monitoring hydrogen peroxide and glucose under relatively low overpotential based on PEI-functionalized metal–organic framework (P-MOF) supported gold nanoparticles/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (AuNPs/N-GQDs) and glucose oxidase. AuNPs/N-GQDs with high peroxidase mimicking activity was anchored on the P-MOF modified electrode surface as H2O2 sensors, exhibiting a high sensitivity of 134.26 μA mM−1 cm−2 and a detection limit of 3.38 μM. Subsequently, AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF used as a nanocarrier for glucose oxidase (GOx) to achieve “cascade nanoreactor” amplification for glucose detection. The amplified amperometric glucose biosensor showed excellent anti-interference ability, reproducibility, and a limit of detection as low as 0.7 μM (S/N = 3) with high sensitivity of 1512.4 μA mM−1 cm−2. In addition, the feasibility of the electrochemical biosensor for accurate and quantitative detection of glucose in human serum samples was confirmed with a recovery ranging from 93.2 to 99.3%. The strategy of combining AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF and glucose oxidase for cascade catalysis in this work provides a simple yet efficient approach to prepare nanomaterial-based enzyme-mimics and enzymes hybrids with high performance, which holds great potential in biological and chemical applications of electrochemical devices and biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enzyme immobilization
Cascade catalysis
AuNPs/N-GQDs
Glucose detection
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the modified electrodes and the electrochemical sensing of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
Detection of glucose in human serum samples
Electrochemical behavior of the fabricated sensor
Glucose sensing performance of the biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images, HRTEM images and the enlarge HRTEM images of the prepared (a–c) N-GQDs and (d–f) AuNPs/N-GQDs. Insets in (b) and (e) show the FFT patterns of N-GQDs and Au NPs/N-GQDs, respectively. (g) XPS survey spectra and (h) XRD patterns of the N-GQDs and the Au NPs−N-GQDs.

Steady state kinetics assay of AuNPs/N-GQDs by varying concentration of (a) TMB and (b) H2O2. The TMB concentration was varied from 0.025 to 1.25 mM, and the H2O2 concentration was varied from 0.5 to 5 mM. Double reciprocal plots of reaction speed with concentration of (c) TMB and (d) H2O2. The results correspond to the mean values and the error bars represent the standard deviation obtained in three parallel measurements.

(a) EIS and (b) CVs of GCE, P-MOF@GCE, AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE, and GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE in 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) solution containing 0.1 M KCl. The inset shows the Randles-equivalent circuit of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. (c) CVs of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE in the absence (black curves) and presence (red curves) of 2 mM H2O2 in N2-saturated 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.0). (d) CVs of GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE in the absence (black curves) and presence (red curves) of 2 mM glucose in untreated 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.0) For Caption: (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(a) Amperometric i-t curves of AuNPs/N-GQDs@GCE and AuNPs/N-GQDs- P-MOF@GCE at −0.3 V with successive addition of H2O2. Insert shows the enlarge view of i-t curves at low H2O2 level. (b) The corresponding calibration curves of AuNPs/N-GQDs@GCE and AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE between response current and H2O2 concentration.

(a) Amperometric i-t curve of GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE at −0.3 V with successive addition of glucose. (b) The corresponding calibration curve of GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs- P-MOF@GCE between response current and glucose concentration. Insert shows the enlarge view of the linear calibration curve in glucose concentration range of 2 μM to 10 μM.

(a) Amperometric current response obtained by the sensor for successive adding glucose, ascorbic acid, sucrose, maltose, d-fructose, glutathione, uric acid, and acetaminophen, respectively. (b) i–t curves of five modified electrodes obtained in the electrolyte (pH 4.0, acetate buffer) with 2 mM glucose. Inset shows the current values of the five parallel tests.
Illustration of the preparation procedure and sensing mechanism of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF based amperometric sensor for H2O2 and glucose.
Detection of glucose in human serum samples by the constructed biosensor.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 1000 times diluted original serum sample is achieved.

head.ce:title: Cascade amplification based on PEI-functionalized metal–organic framework supported gold nanoparticles/nitrogen–doped graphene quantum dots for amperometric biosensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCollege of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Robust and efficient multimodal catalysis platforms that possess high catalytic activity and enhanced electrical conductivity are preferred for enhancing the electrical analysis performance under a low overpotential. This report describes a function-switchable amperometric sensor for electrochemical monitoring hydrogen peroxide and glucose under relatively low overpotential based on PEI-functionalized metal–organic framework (P-MOF) supported gold nanoparticles/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (AuNPs/N-GQDs) and glucose oxidase. AuNPs/N-GQDs with high peroxidase mimicking activity was anchored on the P-MOF modified electrode surface as H2O2 sensors, exhibiting a high sensitivity of 134.26 μA mM−1 cm−2 and a detection limit of 3.38 μM. Subsequently, AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF used as a nanocarrier for glucose oxidase (GOx) to achieve “cascade nanoreactor” amplification for glucose detection. The amplified amperometric glucose biosensor showed excellent anti-interference ability, reproducibility, and a limit of detection as low as 0.7 μM (S/N = 3) with high sensitivity of 1512.4 μA mM−1 cm−2. In addition, the feasibility of the electrochemical biosensor for accurate and quantitative detection of glucose in human serum samples was confirmed with a recovery ranging from 93.2 to 99.3%. The strategy of combining AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF and glucose oxidase for cascade catalysis in this work provides a simple yet efficient approach to prepare nanomaterial-based enzyme-mimics and enzymes hybrids with high performance, which holds great potential in biological and chemical applications of electrochemical devices and biosensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is widely used in chemical industry, food processing industry and pharmaceutical field as a bleaching agent, fungicide, preservative due to its strong oxidizing and reducing properties [1,2]. In addition, H2O2 also plays vital roles in biological systems as one of the major reactive oxygen species [3]. Generally, monitoring H2O2 in the above-mentioned physiological and chemical conditions poses great importance for preventing human body tissue damage since excessive H2O2 disturbs DNA genetic molecule synthesis and causes neuropsychiatric disorders [4]. Moreover, H2O2 is a byproduct of the glucose oxidation process. It is well known that glucose is an essential component of the human body, which provides energy for cell activity and metabolism. However, the excess level of glucose in the blood can result in diabetes mellitus, which is associated with many complications including manifestations in the kidneys, eyes, blood vessels, feet and nerves [5]. Therefore, it is essential and meaningful to close monitoring the concentration levels of H2O2 and glucose under physiological conditions.

Inspired by biological catalysis, artificial enzymes with enzyme-mimicking activities are currently attracted extensive attention in the applications of biosensing [6,7], biological therapeutic agents [8], tumor therapies [9,10], and environmental protection [11]. Up to now, various nanomaterial-based enzyme-mimics have been constructed to meet the growing needs in H2O2 determination [12,13] and glucose monitoring [14,15]. For example, Ma et al. [16] proposed an amplified electrochemical H2O2 sensor based on 2D Cu-TCPP MOF/G4-hemin DNAzyme nanocomposite. Surprisingly, the synergistic effect of interaction between 2D Cu-TCPP MOFs with peroxidase-like activity and G4-hemin DNAzymes can effectively amplify the electrochemical signals and result in a 10 times higher sensitivity than that previously reported. In another study, Shen et al. [17] a multimodal catalytic platform for ultrasensitive glucose detection based on oligonucleotide-functionalized enzymes chemisorbing on magnetic layered double hydroxides. Although significant progress is being made, the applications of enzyme-mimics as H2O2 and glucose sensing platform still face many challenges. The priority one is to find effective ways to balance the biocatalytic performances and the surface functionalization of enzyme-mimics in diverse physiological environments [18], which could offer the optimal biocatalytic performances and sensing performances. Besides, the potential for electrochemical detecting H2O2 or glucose based on such enzyme-mimics requires a relatively high overpotential, at which some electroactive species coexisting in biological fluids can also be oxidized or reduced simultaneously. In addition, the controllable fabrication of nanostructures on electrochemical substrates is still a huge challenge for further practical applications. Thus, facile and versatile strategies to fabricate robust and efficient multimodal catalysis platforms that possess high catalytic activity and enhanced electrical conductivity are preferred for enhancing the electrical analysis performance under a low overpotential.

Thanks to their large surface area-to-volume ratio, interface-dominated properties and the ability to enhance the electrode conductivity and facilitate the electron transfer, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) feature catalytic activity and can decrease the overpotentials of many electroanalytical reactions and maintain the reversibility of redox reactions [19], which make them highly valuable for electrode modification to construct electrocatalyic and electrochemical enzymatic sensors. For instance, AuNPs have been used as electrochemical enhancers for electrochemiluminescence cytosensor [20], photoelectrochemical immunosensor [21], electrochemical aptasensor [22], and electrochemical enzymatic sensor [23]. Early study has demonstrated that surfactant-free gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (AuNPs−N-GQDs) with predominantly exposed (111) facets exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity for electrochemical detection of H2O2 at a relatively low overpotential, which exhibits promising application in physiological and pathological studies [24]. Nevertheless, only by using such gold-based electrocatalytic for further constructing glucose biosensor is not good enough for practical applications.

Recently, metal−organic frameworks (MOFs) used as efficient supports for catalysts immobilization and activity regulation have gained significant interest due to their fascinating properties, such as tunable porosity, large specific surface area, high mass-transfer capacity, controllable functionalities, high density and uniformly dispersed active sites, etc. [25–27]. In addition to the benefit of catalysts immobilization, MOFs provide excellent functions to load with catalytically active metal nanoparticles and to facilitate the contact between active sites and the substrate, which are expected to enhance the signal amplifying ability and catalytic efficiency [28,29]. In our previous work [30], we demonstrated that polyethyleneimine (PEI)-functionalized Fe-based metal–organic framework (P-MOF) provides a large surface area for AuNPs loading and facilitates the contact between uniformly dispersed active sites and the electrode surface. Inspired by this, P-MOF can be used as a suitable support for gold-based electrocatalytic materials, which holds great promise for forming a simple, sensitive, and efficient scheme of electrochemical enzymatic sensing under relatively low overpotential.

As shown in Scheme 1, an amplified amperometric sensor for monitoring H2O2 was developed based on P-MOF supported gold nanoparticles/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF). In addition, taking advantages of high peroxidase-like activity and large specific surface area of AuNPs/N-GQDs, a cascade reaction system for glucose detection has been constructed by immobilizing glucose oxidase (GOx) on AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF surface. The intermediate by-product H2O2 generated from the enzymatic glucose oxidation can be decomposed into H2O and oxygen by the catalysis of AuNPs/N-GQDs, in turn the oxygen can be re-used in the enzymatic glucose oxidation to increase the reaction rate. Therefore, a simple yet efficient cascade catalysis platform for electrochemical monitoring glucose has been successfully constructed based on AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF and GOx.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), citric acid, urea, H2O2, ascorbic acid, sucrose, maltose, D-fructose, glutathione, uric acid, and acetaminophen were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glucose and glucose oxidase (GOx) were purchased from Shanghai MACKLIN Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All the chemicals used to prepare the electrolyte solutions were of analytical grade and obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (> 18.2 MΩ cm) from a Sartorius Arium mini water purification system (Göttingen, Germany). P-MOF was prepared and used as our previous work [30]. Preparation of N-GQDs and AuNPs/ N-GQDs, and mimetic enzymatic activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs composite are offered in the Supporting Information.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was obtained using a FEI TECNAI G2 F20 S-TWIN electron microscope (FEI, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) measurements were carried out by a Zeiss Sigma 500 electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) equipped with monochromatic Al Kα excitation. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was obtained using a D8 advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) operated at 40 kV and 20 mA. Electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China) using a three-electrode system at room temperature. A glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) modified by the prepared materials was used as working electrode. A platinum wire and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) (in saturated KCl solution) were used as counter and reference electrode, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammogram (CV) was measured in 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) solution containing 0.1 M KCl. Amperometric i-t curves for H2O2 and glucose detection were obtained at −0.3 V in 0.1 M N2-saturated acetate buffer (pH 4.0) and untreated acetate buffer (pH 4.0), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the modified electrodes and the electrochemical sensing of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the modification, the bare GCE was sequentially polished with 0.3- and 0.05 μm alumina powder and then washed thoroughly with absolute alcohol and ultrapure water and dried with high-purity nitrogen steam. AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF modified GCE (denoted as AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE) was prepared as follows: 5 μL of P-MOF (1 mg mL−1) was first drop-casted onto the electrode surface and dried under an infrared light source, then, 10 μL of AuNPs/N-GQDs suspension (1 mg mL−1) was dropped on the P-MOF-modified electrode surface and drying in air. For comparing, The AuNPs/N-GQDs modified GCE (denoted as AuNPs/N-GQDs@GCE) was prepared under the same condition without P-MOF casting. Glucose oxidase modified AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE (denoted as GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF @GCE) was prepared through immobilizing GOx on the surface of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE. Typically, 5 μL of a solution of GOx in PBS (20 mg mL−1) containing BSA (20 mg mL−1) as enzyme stabilizer was dropped on AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE surface and incubated for 30 min at room temperature. Then, 3 μL of 2 wt% glutaraldehyde was added and incubated for 60 min at room temperature. Lastly, the modified electrode surface was rinsed with ultrapure water to remove the unreacted glutaraldehyde and coated with 3 μL of Nafion (0.5% in ethanol) to prevent the loss of GOx. Finally, GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE was stored at 4 °C for further use.

AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE and GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE were used for the electrochemical detection of H2O2 and glucose, respectively. The sensor was rinsed with ultrapure water after each measurement. Amperometric i-t curve was conducted at −0.3 V in 20 mL of stirred acetate buffer solution to record the corresponding current via adding different concentrations of H2O2 or glucose. N2-saturated acetate buffer solution was used to avoid the O2 interference for H2O2 monitoring between each measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serums (with known glucose concentrations) were supplied from the Guangzhou Red Cross Hospital. Generally, the standard titration method was conducted to detect glucose in human serum samples under optimized i–t conditions. Specifically, 20 µL of certain concentrations of serum samples were added to 20 mL of stirred acetate buffer solution containing 1 mM glucose and standard concentration of glucose was spiked into the solution. In order to ensure the glucose detection is in the linear interval, 1 mM glucose was first added into the buffer solution before adding serum samples.

The morphology of the prepared N-GQDs and AuNPs/N-GQDs was characterized by TEM as displayed in Fig. 1 a–f. The diameters of the N-GQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 2–3.5 nm with an average diameter of 2.52 nm (Figs. 1a,b and S1a). The fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (Inset in Fig. 1b) and enlarge HRTEM image (Fig. 1c) of the N-GQDs indicate high crystallinity of the N-GQDs, with a lattice parameter of 0.20 nm, corresponding to the lattice fringes of graphene. It can be seen that the AuNPs/N-GQDs present an irregular polyhedral structure with an average diameter of 26.4 nm (Figs. 1d,e and S1b), and cubic crystal structure is observed from the corresponding FFT pattern (Inset in Fig. 1e) of the AuNPs/N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1f, the surface of the AuNPs/N-GQDs is mainly composed of exposed Au (111) facets with a lattice parameter of 0.23 nm in addition to the lattice fringes from N-GQDs. The TEM characterizations clearly displayed the size and morphology changes between the as-synthesized N-GQDs and AuNPs/N-GQDs, which indicate the successfully formation of gold nanoparticles on the surface of the N-GQDs.

The compositions and crystal structures of the N-GQDs and AuNPs/N-GQDs were then characterized by XPS (Fig. 1g) and XRD (Fig. 1h), respectively. It can be clearly seen that there are three predominant peaks at 285.8, 400.0, and 531.9 eV in both XPS spectra of N-GQDs and AuNPs/N-GQDs, corresponding to the C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Compared to the spectrum of N-GQDs, additional three peaks at around 85.3, 335.7, and 354.3 eV is observed in the spectrum of AuNPs/N-GQDs, corresponding to the binding energies of Au4f, Au4d5, and Au4d3. These data indicate that metallic state of the formed gold nanoparticles attached on the N-GQDs. The high-resolution Au4f, C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of AuNPs/N-GQDs and high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra of N-GQDs are further shown and illustrated in Fig. S2. The XPS results clearly demonstrate that nitrogen atoms have been doped into the framework of GQDs with rich N and O elements, and AuNPs/N-GQDs have been successfully prepared by in-situ reduction of AuCl4− on N-GQDs surface. As shown in Fig. 1h, the XRD pattern of AuNPs/N-GQDs exhibits several diffraction peaks at 2θ = 38.21°, 44.50°, 64.78°, 77.66° and 81.76°, which can be assigned to diffractions from the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) facets of face-centered cubic gold. It should be noted that a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.60°, which is attributed to the graphite (002) crystal plane of N-GQDs, is both observed in XRD patterns of AuNPs/N-GQDs and N-GQDs. The XRD characterizations further demonstrate that face-centered cubic gold nanoparticles are successfully formed on the surface of N-GQDs.

To verify the catalytic activity of the AuNPs/N-GQDs composite for H2O2 decomposition, the peroxidase-like activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs in the TMB−H2O2 system was investigated. We first checked the effect of solution pH on peroxidase-like activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs, and observed that the maximum catalytic activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs is reached at pH 4.0 for the TMB−H2O2 system (Fig. S3a and b), which is consistent with those of HRP and several other peroxidase-like nanoenzymes [31]. In addition, the effect of the mass ratio of AuNPs and N-GQDs on the peroxidase-like activity was evaluated to obtain the optimal composite (Fig. S3c and S3d), and results revealed that the peroxidase-like activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs gradually increased as the N-GQDs mass ratio increased from 0.25 to 1.00. The higher peroxidase-like activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs-100 may be ascribed to the more exposed Au (111) facets compared to the others. As shown in Fig. S4, the formed AuNPs/N-GQDs exhibit larger irregular crystal clusters with the decrease of N-GQDs mass ratio, which means a reduction in specific surface area. Thus, AuNPs/N-GQDs-100 with smaller size was applied to construct the electrochemical sensor in our work.

The kinetic parameters of the peroxidase-like reactions were further measured to explore peroxidase-like catalytic properties of AuNPs/N-GQDs. The kinetics data were obtained by varying the concentration of the TMB or H2O2 while keeping one other substrate concentrations constant. The catalytic behavior of AuNPs/N-GQDs toward TMB and H2O2 corresponded to the typical Michaelis–Menten curves (Fig. 2 a and b). The apparent Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and maximum reaction rate (Vmax) for AuNPs/N-GQDs were obtained by fitting the data to Lineweaver−Burk plots (Fig. 2c and d). As indicated in Table S1, the Km value of AuNPs/N-GQDs toward TMB and H2O2 were 0.056 and 0. 469 mM, respectively. Notably, these values are much lower than that of HRP and previous representative enzyme mimics, which indicating a stronger affinity of AuNPs/N-GQDs toward TMB and H2O2 molecules. In addition to the small Km value, AuNPs/N-GQDs exhibited Vmax values of 7.98 × 10−8 M s−1and 4.46 × 10−8 M s−1 to TMB and H2O2, respectively. These results confirms good peroxidase mimicking activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs since small Km and high Vmax generally ensure strong affinity with the substrates, as well as high catalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of the fabricated sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the successful fabrication of the electrochemical sensing platform, the electrochemical performance of the electrode surface during each step of modification was characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammograms (CV). Fig. 3 a displays the Nyquist plots for stepwise fabrication stages of the electrode, where the semicircular diameter at higher frequency range represents the electron transfer resistance (Ret), corresponding to the electron transfer process. By fitting to the Randles-equivalent circuit (inset in Fig. 3a and indicated in Table S2), the bare GCE (curve GCE) exhibits an electron transfer resistance (Ret) value of 183.00 Ω. The Ret value after modifying P-MOF (curve P-MOF@GCE) is significantly reduced to 90.16 Ω, which may be ascribed to the high mass transfer capability and high surface activity of P-MOF. The further modification of AuNPs/N-GQDs (curve AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE) results in a smaller Ret value of 57.19 Ω due to the excellent conductivity of AuNPs/N-GQDs. These data suggest that AuNPs/N-GQDs anchored on P-MOF surface easily helps to facilitate the electron transfer. When BSA stabilized GOx was immobilized on the surface of the electrode (curve GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF @GCE), the Ret value was significantly increased to 803.5 Ω. This is because the protein and enzyme molecules may cause blockages on the electrode surface, and it becomes quite difficult for [Fe(CN)6]3-/4− redox probes to reach the electrode surface. These results indicate that GOx and AuNPs/N-GQDs have successfully incorporated with P-MOF on electrode surface.

As shown in Fig. 3b, the CV curves of different modified electrodes exhibited a pair of well-distinct redox characteristic peaks. After GCE decorated with P-MOF (curve P-MOF@GCE), the redox peak currents increased and the ΔEp is significantly reduced with positive shifts of oxidation peak potential and reduction peak potential compared to bare GCE (curve GCE), which is mainly due to the porous structures of P-MOF helps the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4− redox probe diffuse to the electrode surface and increases the active surface area to provide more reaction sites. The modification of AuNPs/N-GQDs on P-MOF@GCE (curve AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE) further increased the redox peak currents and resulted in negative shifts of oxidation peak potential and reduction peak potential because AuNPs/N-GQDs with good electrical conductivity accelerated the electron transfer. As expected, when the introduction of GOX on AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@ GCE (curve GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@ GCE), there has been an obvious decrease in the redox peak currents due to the hindrance of GOx membrane to electron transfer between [Fe(CN)6]3-/4− and the electrode surface. According to the Randles-Sevcik equation, the active surface area of bare GCE, P-MOF@GCE, AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE and GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE was calculated to be 0.0867, 0.118, 0.108 and 0.0103 cm2, respectively (Fig. S5). These CV results agree with EIS results, and also suggested the successful formation of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE and GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE.

In addition, the electrochemical behaviors of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE toward H2O2 reduction and GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE toward glucose oxidation were investigated. Fig. 3c shows the CVs of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE in the absence (black curves) and presence (red curves) of 2 mM H2O2 in N2-saturated 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Clearly, AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE showed a pronounced current response to H2O2 with onset potential negative than 0 V and significantly increased reduction current was obtained at overpotential negative than −0.3 V. Fig. 3d shows the CVs of GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE in the absence (black curves) and presence (red curves) of 2 mM glucose in untreated 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Similarly, GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE also showed a pronounced current response to glucose with onset potential negative than 0 V and significantly decreased reduction current was obtained at overpotential negative than −0.3 V. It is worth noting that clearly opposed current response for H2O2 and glucose detection was observed, indicating the different changes of O2 content in the electrode interface. In order to ensure high current density and avoid the interference from other biologically active molecules, −0.3 V was chosen for amperometric detection of H2O2 and glucose.

Before carrying out electrochemical detection, pH value of electrolyte solution for the sensor system was optimized. Fig. S6a shows i-t determination response of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE for adding the same amount of H2O2 in electrolyte with different pH values. As seen from Fig. S6a, the current response increases rapidly with the decrease of pH value from 8.0 to 5.0, and no significant increase is observed when further reducing pH value. Electrolyte with pH 4.0 was selected for electrochemical detection of H2O2 since lower current fluctuation was obtained. Fig. S6b shows i-t determination response of GOx-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE for adding the same amount of glucose in electrolyte with different pH values. As expected, the current response increases as reducing pH values, and reaches a plateau at 4.0, further decrease pH value has scarcely additional positive effect (Fig. S6b). Thus, glucose sensing of the biosensor was also conducted in electrolyte with pH 4.0.

Apart from the excellent peroxidase mimicking activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs, the electrocatalytic properties of AuNPs/N-GQDs for H2O2 decomposition were evaluated by amperometric i-t curve. Fig. 4 a shows the typical i–t response curves of AuNPs/N-GQDs@GCE and AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE with the consecutive addition of H2O2 at different concentrations in 20.0 mL of 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.0) at a potential of −0.3 V. It can be seen that the response currents of AuNPs/N-GQDs@GCE and AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE both went up in the shape of the stair step with the H2O2 level increased, and achieved the steady-state current within 1 s, revealing the excellent electrocatalytic performance of AuNPs/N-GQDs in reducing H2O2. Fig. 4b presents the fitted calibration plots between current response and H2O2 concentration, and two linear ranges for H2O2 monitoring are illustrated. The AuNPs/N-GQDs@GCE exhibits satisfactory responsiveness toward H2O2 with two linear ranges of 0.01 mM to 1 mM (I (μA) = –10.21 C[H2O2](mM) – 5.33, R2 = 0.993) and 2 mM to 10 mM (I (μA) = –5.89 C[H2O2](mM) – 12.06, R2 = 0.998), while the AuNPs/N-GQDs- P-MOF@GCE showed two linear ranges for H2O2 of 0.005 mM to 1 mM (I (μA) = –14.50 C[H2O2](mM) – 8.28, R2 = 0.990) and 2 mM to 10 mM (I (μA) = –7.50 C[H2O2](mM) – 20.34, R2 = 0.992). The two linear range parts are associated with the reaction rate of H2O2 at electrodes. Under low level of H2O2, AuNPs/N-GQDs on electrode surface offers sufficient active sites in contact with H2O2, resulting in speed up reactions with a larger linear slope. With a higher concentration of H2O2, the reduction of H2O2 on the electrode surface will slow down and obtain decreased linear slope due to insufficient active sites. The detection limits of AuNPs/N-GQDs@GCE and AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE were evaluated as 9.60 μM and 3.38 μM, respectively (signal-to-noise ratio is defined as 3 times). In addition, according to the formula: sensitivity = S/A, where S is the slope of the linear regression equation and A is the area of the electrode, the sensitivity of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE is calculated to be 134.26 μA mM−1 cm−2, which is 1.42 times of AuNPs/N-GQDs@GCE. These data indicate that the modification of P-MOF significantly enhanced the electrocatalytic performance of AuNPs/N-GQDs. The lower detection limit and higher sensitivity of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE is mainly caused by the high charge transfer capability of porous P-MOF. The interface morphology of AuNPs/N-GQDs and AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF on electrode surface was further investigated by SEM to explain the reason for this amplified electrochemical signal by P-MOF (Fig. S7). It is clear that AuNPs/N-GQDs are well anchored on the P-MOF surface to form a porous coating on the electrode surface. Such surface-clean AuNPs/N-GQDs supported on P-MOF are beneficial for their applications in electrochemical sensing and electrocatalysis due to the synergistic effect between high surface activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs and high-mass transfer rate of P-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose sensing performance of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the excellent electrochemical detection performance of AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE for H2O2, an electrochemical glucose sensor was established. The quantitative analytical performance of the prepared biosensor was investigated by monitoring the current responses for successive injection of glucose at −0.3 V. Fig. 5 a shows the amperometric i-t curve of the biosensor acquired during interactions with increasing glucose concentrations. Surprisingly, the biosensor exhibits gradually decreased response current when low concentration of glucose was added while promptly decreased after adding higher concentration of glucose, and the response of steady-state current is achieved within 5 s. When the concentration of glucose in the solution exceeded 4 mM, the apparent current response was difficult to appear, which indicates that the sensor cannot accurately measure glucose above this concentration. The reason for the clearly opposed response current of glucose is that GOX-AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF@GCE catalyzed the oxidation of glucose and consumed the dissolved oxygen, which result in a significant decline of the current from the oxidation−reduction reaction. A cascade reaction for the oxidation−reduction reaction was supposed to take place on the surface of GCE, and the mechanism of this process is illustrated in Scheme 1. The intermediate by-product H2O2 generated from the enzymatic glucose oxidation can be reduced to H2O and produce oxygen under the catalysis of AuNPs/N-GQDs, and the oxygen in turn can be re-used in the glucose oxidation reaction, which increased the reaction rates of the enzymatic glucose oxidation.

To evaluate the selectivity of the biosensor, the chronological amperometric method was applied to study the potential impact of major interfering compounds such as ascorbic acid, sucrose, maltose, D-fructose, glutathione, uric acid, and acetaminophen on glucose detection. As shown in Fig. 6
a, the sensor showed a significant change in the response current when 1.0 mM glucose was added while had no significant current response when 2.0 mM ascorbic acid, sucrose, maltose, and d-fructose were sequentially added. Then, a significant step was generated again when 1.0 mM glucose was added again while no significant current response occurred when 2.0 mM glutathione was sequentially added. The same phenomenon was further observed when 0.5 mM glucose, 2.0 mM uric acid and acetaminophen were sequentially added. These data indicate that the presence of these substances has substantially no interference with the electrochemical response signal of glucose, and this sensor has excellent anti-interference ability. To evaluate the reproducibility of the biosensor, i-t curves of five modified electrodes were compared in the electrolyte (pH 4.0, acetate buffer) with 2 mM glucose. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, no obvious changes of the current response were found, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the current obtained by five parallel tests is calculated as 3.60%, suggesting that the as-prepared sensor has good repeatability. In addition, the stability of the sensor was investigated by storing the prepared modified electrodes at 4 °C for 15 days. The current responses to 2.0 mM glucose were measured under the same test conditions every 5 days as shown in Table S4. The amperometric response of the sensor after 15 days was decreased 10% of the initial response current, indicating good long-term stability to glucose detection.

To explore the actual diagnostic ability of the constructed biosensor, the standard titration method was performed to detect glucose levels in human serum samples with known glucose concentrations. As shown in Fig. S8, amperometric i-t curve was conducted in 20 mL of acetate buffer (pH 4.0) containing 1 mM glucose, and 20 µL of certain concentration of serum sample were then added in, and standard concentration of glucose was further spiked into the solution. It can be seen that promptly decreased electrochemical response signal was generated upon the addition of serum samples and glucose, and theses amperometric responses of these samples are obviously consistent. As illustrated in Table 1 , the concentration of glucose in serum samples was finally calculated using the linear relationship obtained in Fig. 5b. The results show that the recovery of spiked glucose to the serum sample is range from 93.2 to 99.3%, indicating that the sensor can be successfully used for the detection of glucose in biological samples.

A simple amperometric biosensor for sensitive detection of H2O2 and glucose was developed based on AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF. Surface-clean AuNPs/N-GQDs with high peroxidase mimicking activity were well anchored on the P-MOF surface to form a porous coating on the electrode surface. The synergistic effect between high surface activity of AuNPs/N-GQDs and high-mass transfer rate of P-MOF significantly enhanced the electrochemical sensing and electrocatalytic abilities of the sensor. In addition, AuNPs/N-GQDs-P-MOF was used as a nanocarrier for glucose oxidase to achieve “cascade nanoreactor” amplification for glucose detection. The amperometric biosensor exhibited excellent selectivity, stability and reproducibility, together with outstanding performance in human serum sample analysis, indicating its great potential in biological and chemical applications of electrochemical devices and biosensing. This work provides a simple yet efficient strategy to construct multimodal catalysis platforms that possess high catalytic activity and enhanced electrical conductivity for enhancing the electrical analysis performance under a low overpotential.

Youxiong Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xianhu Wei: Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Qihui Gu: Validation, Visualization. Jumei Zhang: Supervision, Validation. Yu Ding: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Liang Xue: Validation, Data curation. Moutong Chen: Validation, Data curation. Juan Wang: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Shi Wu: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xiaojuan Yang: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Shuhong Zhang: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Tao Lei: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Qingping Wu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships which have or could be perceived to have influenced the work reported in this article.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (31901783); Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2019M652835); Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory (2020B121201009) and GDAS' Project of Science and Technology Development (2019GDASYL-0103006).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.139803.
